questions.question: A web application calls a stored procedure using the following logic: EXEC GetCustomerDetails ' + userInput The developer argues that stored procedures automatically prevent SQL injection. During testing, specially crafted input changes the procedure's intended query. What is the most accurate conclusion?
options: Stored procedures prevent injection as long as the database account has limited privileges
Stored procedures remain vulnerable when untrusted input is concatenated into dynamic SQL
Stored procedures are vulnerable only when the application uses HTTP GET
The weakness is primarily broken access control because a database procedure was invoked
questions.description: Stored procedures are not automatically secure. If untrusted input is concatenated into dynamically constructed SQL, the resulting statement can still be manipulated. Parameterized stored-procedure calls are safer because user input is treated as data rather than executable SQL syntax.

questions.question: A web application loads templates using this request: /view?template=invoice.html A tester changes the value to a path referencing a sensitive local operating-system file, and the server returns that file's contents. Which vulnerability is most directly demonstrated?
options: Remote File Inclusion because an external resource was requested
Local File Inclusion because a server-local file was included through user-controlled input
Directory listing because the server revealed a folder structure
Path disclosure because the application exposed an absolute path in an error message
questions.description: The application uses user-controlled input to load a file that exists locally on the server. That is Local File Inclusion. Remote File Inclusion requires the application to load code or content from a remote location.

questions.question: A PHP application accepts a URL in a `page` parameter and downloads the referenced content before executing it as part of the application. Which risk is most directly present?
options: Remote File Inclusion
Local File Inclusion
Server-Side Request Forgery only
Stored Cross-Site Scripting
questions.description: When an application accepts a remote URL and includes or executes the fetched content, an attacker may supply malicious remote code. Although remote fetching can also resemble SSRF, the decisive fact here is that the fetched content is included and executed by the application.

questions.question: A tester submits 50 MB of deeply nested JSON to an API endpoint. The server spends excessive CPU time parsing the request and becomes unavailable, even though no unusually large number of requests is sent. Which weakness most directly enabled the attack?
options: Broken authentication because the request was accepted
Insufficient input validation and resource-consumption controls
SQL injection because structured input reached the server
Network-layer DDoS because the service became unavailable
questions.description: The attack abuses one computationally expensive request rather than overwhelming the server with distributed traffic. The application should restrict input size, nesting depth, parsing complexity, execution time, and resource consumption.

questions.question: A C program copies a 200-byte username into a 64-byte stack buffer without checking its length. The program crashes, and an adjacent return address is overwritten. Which control would most directly prevent the underlying coding flaw?
options: Server-side authorization checks
Bounds checking before copying input into the buffer
Encrypting the input before storing it
Disabling verbose database errors
questions.description: The root cause is writing more data than the destination buffer can hold. Proper bounds checking and memory-safe functions directly prevent this. Other mitigations such as ASLR, stack canaries, and DEP reduce exploitability but do not remove the coding defect.

questions.question: A developer deploys stack canaries, ASLR, and Data Execution Prevention on a vulnerable application but leaves the unsafe copy operation unchanged. Which statement is most accurate?
options: The buffer overflow has been eliminated because three mitigations are active
Exploitation may become harder, but the underlying vulnerability still exists
ASLR converts the overflow into an input-validation issue only
Data Execution Prevention guarantees that control flow cannot be hijacked
questions.description: These protections are exploit mitigations. They make successful exploitation more difficult but do not correct the unsafe memory operation. The vulnerability remains until the application performs safe bounds checking or uses memory-safe handling.

questions.question: A threat-modelling team identifies a payment database, an Internet-facing API, an internal administrator account, and a connection from the API to the database. Which element represents a trust boundary?
options: The payment database because it contains valuable data
The administrator account because it has elevated privileges
The point where requests move from the Internet-facing API into the internal database environment
The encryption key used to secure payment records
questions.description: A trust boundary exists where data or control crosses between areas with different trust levels. The transition from an external-facing component to an internal database zone is therefore a critical trust boundary.

questions.question: During threat modelling, a team identifies an attacker who can modify transaction values while data moves between two components. Which security property is most directly threatened?
options: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
Non-repudiation only
questions.description: Unauthorized modification of data primarily threatens integrity. Confidentiality concerns unauthorized disclosure, while availability concerns disruption of access.

questions.question: A web server allows the HTTP `TRACE` method. A tester sends a request containing custom headers and receives those headers echoed in the response. What is the most accurate security concern?
options: TRACE automatically grants administrative access
TRACE may expose request information and can support Cross-Site Tracing in certain environments
TRACE is equivalent to DELETE and can remove server files
TRACE proves that the server is vulnerable to SQL injection
questions.description: TRACE echoes request data for diagnostic purposes. Historically, it could be abused with browser-side weaknesses to expose sensitive headers. It should be disabled when not required, but its presence alone does not prove a complete exploit.

questions.question: A REST API accepts a `PUT` request that replaces an existing user profile. Repeating the same request produces the same final state. Which HTTP property is demonstrated?
options: Safety
Idempotence
Confidentiality
Non-persistence
questions.description: An idempotent operation can be repeated without changing the final result beyond the initial application. PUT is generally intended to be idempotent, although it is not considered safe because it modifies server state.

questions.question: A server permits unauthenticated clients to use the HTTP `DELETE` method against uploaded files. What is the primary vulnerability?
options: Insecure HTTP version negotiation
Broken access control combined with dangerous method exposure
Cross-Site Scripting because a URL deletes content
Buffer overflow because DELETE changes server-side data
questions.description: The dangerous method is not inherently a vulnerability if correctly authorized. The critical failure is allowing unauthenticated or unauthorized users to invoke it.

questions.question: A Burp Suite scan identifies a possible SQL injection point, but the response behaviour is inconsistent because the application uses caching and rate limiting. What should the tester do next?
options: Accept the scanner result as confirmed because automated scanners are deterministic
Manually reproduce the request using Repeater and compare controlled responses
Use Decoder because the issue involves server-side SQL
Use Spider because crawling confirms injection vulnerabilities
questions.description: Automated findings require validation. Burp Repeater allows controlled modification and replay of requests so the tester can distinguish genuine injection behaviour from caching, rate limiting, or random response variation.

questions.question: A tester configures Burp Intruder with two payload positions: one for usernames and one for passwords. Every username is tested against every password. Which attack configuration is conceptually being used?
options: A single-position sequential attack
A Cartesian-product attack across both payload sets
A passive scanner audit
A crawler-based attack-surface mapping operation
questions.description: Testing every value from one payload set against every value from another creates all possible combinations. This is appropriate for controlled credential testing but can generate a very large number of requests.

questions.question: A security team runs a SAST tool on source code and a DAST tool against the deployed application. SAST reports an unsafe function that cannot be reached in the current build, while DAST reports an exposed endpoint whose source is generated at runtime. Which conclusion is most accurate?
options: SAST is wrong because unreachable code can never matter
DAST is wrong because all vulnerabilities must be visible in source code
The tools provide complementary views and require contextual validation
Only DAST results should be reported because they affect a running system
questions.description: SAST can identify weaknesses in code that may be unreachable or inactive, while DAST can find runtime behaviour that static analysis may not understand. Both require manual validation and context.

questions.question: A vulnerability is assigned a CVE identifier, and the NVD provides severity metrics and references. Which statement is most accurate?
options: CVE proves that every product version listed is exploitable
NVD replaces the vendor's security advisory
CVE provides a standardized identifier, while NVD enriches it with analysis and metadata
CVE and NVD are two names for the same database
questions.description: CVE provides standardized identifiers and descriptions. NVD builds upon CVE records with additional information such as CVSS data, references, and affected configurations. Vendor advisories remain important for precise remediation details.

questions.question: A tester observes a browser warning generated by a JavaScript-security add-on after a page loads an obfuscated external script from an unfamiliar domain. What is the add-on most likely helping identify?
options: Suspicious or malicious client-side script behaviour
Kernel-level privilege escalation
SQL query execution inside the database
TCP SYN flooding against the browser
questions.description: A browser JavaScript guard focuses on suspicious scripts, dangerous domains, obfuscation, or browser-side behaviour. It does not replace server-side testing or network intrusion detection.

questions.question: A company's incident occurred because an employee voluntarily disclosed a one-time password after receiving a convincing call from someone claiming to be technical support. Which classification is most accurate?
options: Passive technical attack
Social-engineering attack exploiting human trust
Offline password attack
Network-based credential replay
questions.description: The attacker manipulated the victim rather than breaking the authentication technology. This demonstrates that information security includes human behaviour, awareness, and verification procedures.

questions.question: A security professional discovers a serious vulnerability in a public service and tests it without permission because the goal is to help the organization. How should the action be classified?
options: Ethical hacking because the intent was beneficial
Authorized penetration testing because no data was stolen
Unauthorized activity because ethical intent does not replace consent
Grey-team testing because both offensive and defensive goals were involved
questions.description: Ethical hacking requires explicit authorization, a defined scope, and agreed rules. Good intentions do not make unauthorized testing legal or ethical.

questions.question: A professional security team performs attacks against an organization, while another team monitors, detects, and responds. Afterward, both groups jointly study missed detections and improve controls. Which activity best describes the final collaborative stage?
options: Red teaming
Blue teaming
Purple teaming
Grey-hat hacking
questions.description: Purple teaming combines offensive and defensive perspectives to improve detection and response. It is a collaborative function rather than simply a third independent attack team.

questions.question: An attacker uses a proxy chain primarily to hide the apparent source of requests. A tester then assumes the proxy guarantees complete anonymity. Why is that assumption unsafe?
options: Proxies cannot forward HTTP traffic
Logs, browser fingerprints, DNS leakage, timing correlation, and upstream monitoring may still identify the source
Proxies automatically expose every password to the destination
Proxy chains work only for local network attacks
questions.description: A proxy changes the network path, but it does not guarantee anonymity. Multiple technical and operational indicators can still reveal or correlate the user.

questions.question: A tester observes a user typing a password through an encrypted HTTPS connection. Network capture does not reveal the password, but malware installed on the endpoint records the keystrokes before encryption occurs. Which technique defeated the protection?
options: Passive packet sniffing
Keylogging at the endpoint
Offline hash cracking
HTTP tunnelling
questions.description: HTTPS protects data in transit, not data before it is entered into the encrypted channel. A keylogger captures input at the endpoint before encryption.

questions.question: A malicious application records browsing activity, captures screenshots, and sends the information to a remote operator without providing direct remote command access. Which classification is most accurate?
options: Spyware
Reverse-connecting Trojan only
Worm
File-infector virus
questions.description: Spyware primarily monitors and exfiltrates user or system information. A Trojan may deliver spyware, but the observed behaviour most directly matches spyware.

questions.question: A backdoor transmits stolen data inside the unused fields of legitimate protocol messages so that the communication resembles normal traffic. Which concept is demonstrated?
options: Overt channel
Covert channel
Broadcast amplification
Passive reconnaissance
questions.description: A covert channel uses an unintended or hidden communication mechanism to bypass security controls. An overt channel uses a legitimate communication path for its intended purpose.

questions.question: A reverse-connecting Trojan successfully reaches its operator even though the victim is behind NAT and rejects unsolicited inbound connections. Why is the reverse connection effective?
options: NAT permits all inbound connections initiated by external systems
The infected host initiates an outbound connection that network controls may allow
The Trojan automatically disables every firewall
Reverse connections do not use TCP or UDP
questions.description: Many networks permit outbound connections more readily than inbound ones. Once the victim initiates the session, NAT and stateful firewalls maintain the return path.

questions.question: A workstation begins making periodic connections to an unfamiliar external IP, a new process launches at startup, and a system utility has an altered hash. Which interpretation is strongest?
options: These are independent events and should not be correlated
The combined indicators are consistent with Trojan persistence and command-and-control activity
The evidence proves a worm outbreak across the network
The altered hash confirms that antivirus software caused the change
questions.description: No single indicator proves a Trojan, but persistence, suspicious outbound communication, and modified system files collectively support that conclusion and justify deeper investigation.

questions.question: A virus scanner detects malware by matching a known byte pattern. A modified sample changes its code appearance but preserves the same behaviour and avoids detection. Which limitation is demonstrated?
options: Signature detection may fail when malware changes its observable representation
Behaviour-based detection can detect only known hashes
File integrity monitoring prevents all malware execution
Static analysis automatically executes the malware
questions.description: Signature-based detection depends on known patterns. Polymorphism, packing, encryption, or obfuscation can change those patterns without changing malicious behaviour.

questions.question: An antivirus product allows an unknown program to execute in an isolated environment and flags it after observing file encryption, process injection, and persistence creation. Which detection method is primarily being used?
options: Pure signature matching
Behavioural or heuristic analysis
Password cracking
Network enumeration
questions.description: The program is classified based on suspicious actions rather than a fixed byte signature. Behavioural analysis can detect previously unknown or modified malware.

questions.question: A network uses an old hub instead of a switch. A tester can observe traffic from unrelated hosts without sending forged packets or altering network tables. Which technique best describes the observation?
options: Active sniffing
Passive sniffing
MAC flooding
ARP poisoning
questions.description: On shared media such as a hub, traffic is delivered to all connected systems. The tester can capture it without manipulating the network. Active sniffing is generally required on switched networks.

questions.question: An attacker uses ARP poisoning to position a machine between a victim and the default gateway. However, the attacker forgets to forward packets, and the victim loses connectivity. Which outcome occurred?
options: Successful passive sniffing with no service impact
A man-in-the-middle attempt that also caused denial of service
DNS spoofing without traffic interception
Session fixation without packet modification
questions.description: ARP poisoning redirected the victim's traffic to the attacker. Without forwarding, packets stopped at the attacker, interrupting connectivity and causing a denial-of-service effect.

questions.question: A network administrator configures static ARP entries for critical systems. Which attack is this control most directly intended to reduce?
options: DNS cache poisoning
ARP spoofing on the local network
TCP SYN flooding
Wireless password guessing
questions.description: Static ARP mappings reduce the ability of forged ARP replies to alter IP-to-MAC associations. The control can be operationally difficult at scale but directly addresses ARP poisoning.

questions.question: A web application assigns a session identifier before login and continues using the same identifier after successful authentication. An attacker first forces a victim to use a known identifier and later reuses it. Which attack is demonstrated?
options: Session fixation
Session hijacking through packet sniffing
Credential stuffing
Cross-Site Request Forgery only
questions.description: In session fixation, the attacker predetermines or supplies a session identifier that remains valid after the victim authenticates. Regenerating the session ID after login is a primary defence.

questions.question: A defender enables SYN cookies on a heavily targeted web server. Which attack is the control primarily designed to mitigate?
options: Smurf amplification
SYN-flood exhaustion of the half-open connection queue
HTTP tunnelling
DNS zone-transfer abuse
questions.description: SYN cookies allow the server to avoid storing state for every initial SYN request. This reduces resource exhaustion caused by large numbers of incomplete handshakes.

questions.question: An attacker sends a small request to an intermediary that generates a much larger response toward a victim using a spoofed source address. Which characteristic is central to the attack?
options: Reflection combined with amplification
Session fixation combined with credential reuse
Passive sniffing combined with DNS tunnelling
Trojan wrapping combined with persistence
questions.description: The intermediary reflects responses to the spoofed victim, and the response is larger than the original request. Smurf and some UDP-based DDoS attacks follow this pattern.

questions.question: A wireless access point uses WEP. An attacker captures a large number of packets and exploits repeated initialization vectors to recover the key. What is the primary cryptographic weakness?
options: WEP uses no encryption at all
Reuse of short initialization vectors enables statistical key-recovery attacks
WEP keys are transmitted in plaintext during every association
WEP depends on public-key certificates that can be forged
questions.description: WEP uses RC4 with a short initialization vector. IV reuse creates predictable relationships between encrypted packets, enabling practical statistical attacks.

questions.question: A wireless network hides its SSID and uses MAC filtering but no strong encryption. The administrator claims this prevents unauthorized access. Which assessment is most accurate?
options: The network is secure because both identity values are concealed
SSIDs and MAC addresses can often be observed or spoofed, so strong authentication and encryption are still required
Hidden SSIDs automatically provide WPA-level confidentiality
MAC filtering prevents packet sniffing even when encryption is absent
questions.description: SSID hiding and MAC filtering are weak administrative measures. They do not provide robust confidentiality or authentication and should not replace WPA2/WPA3 with strong credentials.

questions.question: A fingerprint scanner accepts a carefully constructed artificial fingerprint made from a lifted latent print. Which attack has occurred?
options: Credential stuffing
Biometric spoofing
Session replay only
Physical tailgating
questions.description: The attacker presents an artificial biometric characteristic that the sensor accepts as belonging to the legitimate user. Liveness detection and multi-factor authentication can reduce this risk.

questions.question: A monitoring server checks whether hosts, services, disk space, and response times remain within expected thresholds. It alerts administrators when a service becomes unavailable but does not inspect packet payloads for attack signatures. Which tool role is most accurately represented?
options: Nagios-style infrastructure monitoring
Snort-style network intrusion detection
Metasploit exploitation
Nessus vulnerability assessment
questions.description: Nagios focuses on availability, health, and performance of hosts and services. Snort analyzes network traffic for suspicious patterns, while Nessus assesses vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Snort is configured in passive mode and generates an alert for a known attack signature. The malicious packet still reaches the server. Why?
options: Snort cannot detect signatures
Passive IDS deployment observes and alerts but is not positioned to block traffic
The firewall automatically overrides every IDS decision
Signature detection works only after exploitation succeeds
questions.description: To block traffic, the system must operate inline as an IPS or integrate with an enforcement mechanism. Passive monitoring alone does not stop packets.

questions.question: A system administrator deploys USB Pratirodh to control unauthorized use of removable storage devices. Which risk is the tool most directly intended to reduce?
options: Web-based SQL injection
Data theft or malware introduction through USB devices
Wireless MAC spoofing
DNS reconnaissance
questions.description: USB device control reduces risks from unauthorized removable media, including malware delivery, data exfiltration, and uncontrolled storage usage.

questions.question: An organization deploys AppSamvid to permit only approved applications to run on endpoints. Which security model does this most closely implement?
options: Application allowlisting
Network intrusion detection
Dynamic malware sandboxing
Password spraying prevention
questions.description: Application allowlisting permits trusted, approved software and blocks unapproved executables. This is stronger than relying only on signatures of known malicious programs.

questions.question: During Android application development, source code and resources are compiled, converted into DEX bytecode, packaged into an APK, aligned, and digitally signed. Why is signing essential before installation?
options: It converts Java into DEX bytecode
It establishes application identity and protects update continuity
It grants all requested runtime permissions
It encrypts every resource inside the APK
questions.description: Android requires applications to be signed. Updates normally must be signed with the same key, which helps establish continuity of application identity and detect unauthorized replacement.

questions.question: An Android application exposes a `BroadcastReceiver` to every application and performs a sensitive action whenever it receives a particular intent. Which risk is most direct?
options: Another application may send a crafted intent and trigger the sensitive action
The APK signature will automatically become invalid
Android Runtime will stop executing DEX bytecode
SharedPreferences will become world-readable
questions.description: Exported Android components can be invoked by other applications. Sensitive exported receivers should enforce permissions, validate input, and verify the caller where appropriate.

questions.question: An Android activity stores important state only in memory. The operating system destroys and recreates the activity after a configuration change, and the state is lost. Which Android application concept is most directly involved?
options: Activity lifecycle management
APK signing
Root privilege escalation
Network-based MITM
questions.description: Android may pause, stop, destroy, and recreate activities. Applications must preserve necessary state using appropriate lifecycle methods and storage mechanisms.

questions.question: A tester uses ADB to read application logs and discovers authentication tokens printed by the application during normal execution. Which security weakness is most direct?
options: Sensitive information exposure through logging
Weak APK signing
Insecure wireless authentication
Excessive Android permissions only
questions.description: Secrets, tokens, passwords, and personal information should not be written to logs. Logs may be accessible during debugging, through privileged applications, or on compromised devices.

questions.question: A tester decompiles an Android application and finds that root detection checks only for one known file path. Using Frida, the tester hooks the detection method and forces it to return "not rooted." What does the scenario demonstrate?
options: Static root-detection checks can be bypassed through runtime instrumentation
Frida permanently changes the APK signing certificate
Rooting is prevented when Java source is decompiled
APKTool automatically removes Android permissions
questions.description: Client-side security checks can often be inspected and manipulated. Frida can hook methods while the application runs and alter their return values. Root detection should therefore be treated as a risk signal rather than an absolute security boundary.
